preamble: The vowel [a] shifts to [e] or [æ] through fronting, a common process in vowel systems where back vowels advance toward the front of the oral cavity. This occurs frequently as a conditioned change (after palatalized consonants or before front vowels) and less commonly as an unconditioned historical shift.

examples.note: Southeastern Turkic umlaut: /a/ in initial syllable is raised and fronted to /e/ under influence of primary /i/ in subsequent syllable. Also shows palatal-conditioned vowel lowering in final syllable (i → iq).

Verb stem fronting: stem vowel /a/ alternates with /ɛ/ in the 3sg morphological form, a nonconcatenative vowel-change pattern. E.g., /ɣap/ 'to catch' (infinitive) → /ɣɛp/ (3sg). This is one of two complementary vowel alternations (fronting and lowering) that drive the Dinka verbal paradigm.
